summary: This post examines the reentrancy pitfalls of using Application.DoEvents() in Windows Forms applications. A colleague's code sample demonstrates how DoEvents processes all messages in the queue, not just paint messages, leading to unexpected method reentry. The post acknowledges these dangers but ultimately defends DoEvents as a pragmatic middle ground between frozen UIs and complex multithreaded code. Atwood suggests that despite its quirks, DoEvents remains valuable for specific use cases like improving perceived form load performance.
key_points: DoEvents processes ALL messages in the queue, not just paint messages, which causes unexpected reentrancy
Any method called directly or indirectly in response to message queue processing can be reentered during DoEvents
A Form.Busy property that only processes paint messages would be a useful addition to prevent input-related reentrancy
DoEvents represents a pragmatic compromise between unresponsive UIs and full multithreaded complexity
The technique can be valuable for specific scenarios like improving perceived form load performance
money_quotes: DoEvents processes all of the messages in the message queue, not just paint messages.
Always remember that DoEvents can cause methods to be reentered.
I think it's definitely the lesser of two evils: it's either this simplified cooperative yielding or full-bore multithreaded code.
DoEvents can be a big win with minimal effort in the right situations.
key_insight: DoEvents is a dangerous but sometimes worthwhile shortcut—understanding its reentrancy implications lets you use it safely as an alternative to complex threading.
